subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Rate & Accumulation (Table)
stimulus:
The temperature of coffee in a cup at time $t$ minutes is modeled by a decreasing differentiable function $C$, where $C(t)$ is measured in degrees Celsius. Values of $C(t)$ at selected values of $t$ are given in the table below.

| $t$ (minutes) | 0 | 3 | 7 | 12 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C(t)$ (degrees Celsius) | 90 | 82 | 67 | 55 |

For $12 \leq t \leq 20$, the rate of change of the temperature of the coffee is modeled by $C'(t) = -\dfrac{2e^{0.01t}}{\sqrt{t}}$ degrees Celsius per minute.

parts.prompt: Approximate $C'(5)$ using the average rate of change of $C$ over the interval $3 \leq t \leq 7$. Show the work that leads to your answer and include units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Correct units: degrees Celsius per minute

parts.prompt: Use a left Riemann sum with the three subintervals indicated by the data in the table to approximate $\displaystyle\int_0^{12} C(t)\,dt$. Interpret the meaning of $\dfrac{1}{12}\displaystyle\int_0^{12} C(t)\,dt$ in the context of the problem.

scoring_points.description: Correct left Riemann sum form using the three subintervals $[0,3]$, $[3,7]$, $[7,12]$: $C(0) \cdot 3 + C(3) \cdot 4 + C(7) \cdot 5$

Correct contextual interpretation with units: $\dfrac{1}{12}\displaystyle\int_0^{12} C(t)\,dt$ is the average temperature of the coffee, in degrees Celsius, over the time interval from $t = 0$ to $t = 12$ minutes.
common_traps: Not referencing the averaging context (saying 'total' instead of 'average')

parts.prompt: For $12 \leq t \leq 20$, the rate of change of the temperature of the coffee is modeled by $C'(t) = -\dfrac{2e^{0.01t}}{\sqrt{t}}$, where $C'(t)$ is measured in degrees Celsius per minute. Find the temperature of the coffee at time $t = 20$. Show the setup for your calculations.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral setup using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: $C(20) = C(12) + \displaystyle\int_{12}^{20} C'(t)\,dt = 55 + \displaystyle\int_{12}^{20} \left(-\dfrac{2e^{0.01t}}{\sqrt{t}}\right)dt$
common_traps: Forgetting to add the initial value $C(12) = 55$
Using $C(0) = 90$ instead of $C(12) = 55$ as the initial condition

parts.prompt: For the model defined in part (c), it can be shown that $C''(t) = \dfrac{e^{0.01t}(0.02t - 1)}{t^{3/2}}$. For $12 < t < 20$, determine whether the temperature of the coffee is changing at a decreasing rate or at an increasing rate. Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: The temperature is changing at a decreasing rate because $C''(t) < 0$ for $12 < t < 20$. For $t$ in this interval, $0.02t < 0.4 < 1$, so $0.02t - 1 < 0$, making $C''(t) < 0$. Since $C'(t) < 0$ and $C''(t) < 0$, the derivative $C'(t)$ is becoming more negative, meaning the rate of temperature change is decreasing (the coffee is cooling faster).
common_traps: Confusing the sign of $C''(t)$ — $0.02t - 1 < 0$ for $t < 50$, so $C''(t) < 0$ on $[12,20]$
Incorrectly concluding 'increasing rate' because the temperature itself is decreasing
